text:
Uranium recovery from wet phosphoric acid (WPA) is an increasingly important and sustainable route to meet global uranium demands. World uranium extraction from phosphate ores was demonstrated commercially at several operating deposits between 1951 and 1999 but these operations have been shut down due to market condition fluctuations. In this review we thoroughly describe the historic solvent extraction processes used: the Oak Ridge D2EHPA/TOPO, the OPAP and OPPA processes and their underlying technologies, flowsheets and relevant physico-chemical phenomena.

From the abstract: A review of the role of chemistry in uranium extraction processes over the last century highlights ongoing research to address inherent difficulties in the vanadate process, the world’s principal uranium extraction technology. A significant part of the paper is therefore concerned with developments in extractant chemistry recorded over the last twenty years which address a number of long-standing problems including low selectivity towards iron(III) and the attendant decrease in uranium extraction efficiency in highly concentrated phosphoric acid solutions. New developments in extraction chemistry, which are particularly aimed at improving the effectiveness of uranium extraction through the use of bifunctional ligands like alkylcarbamoylnonyl phosphonates to yield higher uranium distribution ratios and purity values, are reported. In addition, new approaches such as using ion exchange resins and extracting rare earth elements in the uranium process are covered to ensure an economically viable WPA processing route. Chemistry in uranium extraction over the last hundred years — past lessons for the future This review explores in some detail the development of uranium extractive metallurgy spanning one hundred years and summarises recent results of the continuing search for improved cost effective uranium extraction processes realisable in the changed scenario of the uranium and energy industries.
explanation: The text uses domain-specific jargon and plausible review structure, which is consistent with human-authored technical writing. However, its repetitive, abstract-like phrasing and lack of citations are traits often seen in AI-generated content.
